era_context: Late Bronze Age (LBA) in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The New Kingdom of Egypt under the 18th Dynasty (Thutmosid period) was at its height; the Hittite Empire dominated Anatolia and northern Syria; Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period; and the Shang Dynasty ruled China. This was an era of intensive inter-regional trade, military competition, and the development of alphabetic writing systems.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Southeast Asia and Oceania (sparse written records)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Reign of Amenhotep II in Egypt (approximate period)
events.description: Amenhotep II (Aakheperenre) ruled Egypt during the mid-18th Dynasty. He is known from inscriptions and temple reliefs as a military commander and hunter. His reign saw continued Egyptian dominance in the Levant and maintenance of the empire established by his predecessors. The exact chronology of his reign remains debated among Egyptologists, with dates ranging across the 1430s–1400s BCE depending on the chronological system employed.

sources.name: Temple inscriptions at Karnak and Amada
Thutmosid administrative records and king lists

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the 18th Dynasty remains contested. Multiple competing chronological systems (high, middle, low) place Amenhotep II's reign within a 30-year window. This date is assigned by convention within the 'middle chronology' framework.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Tudhaliya I (approximate period)
events.description: Tudhaliya I (or Tudhaliya II, depending on historiographic tradition) ruled the Hittite Empire during the mid-Late Bronze Age. He is credited with military campaigns in Syria and the consolidation of Hittite power in Anatolia. His reign represents a period of Hittite expansion and the development of the imperial administrative system. Dating is highly uncertain and debated among scholars.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and clay tablets from Boğazköy

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology is notoriously uncertain. The identity and dating of Tudhaliya I/II is disputed; some scholars merge these figures, others distinguish them. Placement in the 1560s BCE is speculative and depends on which chronological system is adopted.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional assignment of Amenhotep II to the 1430s–1400s BCE is challenged by some scholars who advocate for a 'low chronology' placing him a century earlier. Similarly, Hittite chronology for this period is in flux; recent scholarship has questioned traditional king list sequences, and some events attributed to Tudhaliya I may belong to later rulers or may be legendary elaborations. No contemporary synchronisms between Egyptian and Hittite records exist for this specific year, making cross-dating speculative.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1560 BCE is heavily dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions and Hittite clay tablets, both of which are elite-authored and may emphasize military and political achievements over economic or social realities. The chronological framework itself is contested: Egyptian king lists are not contemporary and were compiled centuries later; Hittite king lists similarly suffer from gaps and duplications. Archaeological evidence (stratigraphy, radiocarbon dating) provides some constraint but remains subject to calibration uncertainty. The historical record is skewed toward elite male actors and military events; women, non-elites, and economic life are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep II's reign spans multiple years within the mid-18th Dynasty period
